design_philosophy.core_loop: Select a character with unique traversal abilities, navigate level hazards and enemies, reach boss encounter, defeat boss to progress world. Rescue Babs Bunny from Montana Max across 6 thematic worlds.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Character ability selection creates tactical depth—choosing the right character (Buster's jump height, Dizzy's wall destruction, Furrball's climbing, Plucky's flight/swimming) for level obstacles vs. maintaining flexibility for upcoming challenges.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Play as Tiny Toon characters with distinct personalities and powers; experience a cartoon rescue adventure with multiple playstyle options within a linear structure.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Konami's design enforces character-switching as core mechanic rather than cosmetic choice. Star ball pickups force mid-level commitment to ability swaps. Elmyra gauntlet sections (level 2 of each world) break combat/platforming with stealth/evasion, creating tonal variety. This reflects cartoon show's episodic, character-focused structure rather than pure platforming mastery.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard NES lives system with extra life acquisition via Hamton NPC (30 carrots = 1 extra life)
difficulty.checkpointing: Worlds reset entirely upon Elmyra capture in level 2; level 3 boss defeats progress to next world. No mid-level checkpoints documented.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection provides tactical difficulty modulation—Dizzy trivializes wall-dependent obstacles; Furrball bypasses vertical gauntlets; Plucky excels in water sections. Carrot collection for extra lives allows resource-driven difficulty smoothing.

progression.upgrade_system: No persistent stat upgrades. Star ball pickups enable temporary character switching. Carrot collection funds extra lives via Hamton (30 carrots per life).
progression.unlock_mechanism: Six worlds unlock sequentially: The Hills → The Wetlands → The Trees → Downtown → Wackyland → Monty's Mansion. First four worlds have 3 levels each (platforming, Elmyra evasion, boss). Final two worlds have 1 level each.
replayability_hooks: Four playable characters with distinct ability sets encourage alt-playthroughs for different skill expression
Character-specific level design optimizations (Dizzy walls, Furrball climbs, Plucky water sections) create varied tactical approaches
Carrot/extra life economy incentivizes thoroughness and score-hunting

world_building_techniques: Thematic world names (The Hills, Wetlands, Trees, Downtown, Wackyland, Monty's Mansion) mirror cartoon episodic locales
Character roster reflects show's cast with ability-driven characterization (Dizzy's destructive spin, Furrball's climbing, Plucky's aerial prowess)
Elmyra Duff encounters as level 2 gauntlet—character-specific stealth challenge reflecting show's Elmyra dynamic
Final boss confrontation with Montana Max (kidnapper) provides narrative payoff

boss_design.philosophy: Each world concludes level 3 with boss battle. Design philosophy appears character-ability responsive—bosses likely exploit specific character matchups or force players to reconsider character selection mid-world.
notable_bosses: Montana Max (Monty) - final antagonist, kidnapper of Babs Bunny

danger_signals: Elmyra Duff (predatory character, enforces world-reset penalty)
environmental_storytelling: World progression through Tiny Toon themed environments (rural hills, swamps, forests, urban centers, surreal Wackyland)
Monty's Mansion as final world reinforces class/wealth gap narrative (wealthy antagonist hideaway)
Hamton's NPC role as carrot-to-life converter establishes supply chain economy

sources_used: wikipedia - Tiny Toon Adventures (video game): Gameplay section detailing character abilities, world structure, Elmyra mechanics, and Hamton NPC
data_crystal - Technical ROM specs confirming Konami development, release dates, and platform architecture
